1: ki hinneh bayyamim hahem uvʾet hahi ʾasher ʾashiv ʾet shĕvut Yĕhudah wĕYĕrushalayyim: “For behold, in those days and at that time, when I restore the fortunes of Judah and Jerusalem.” Shiv ʾet shĕvut (restore the fortunes) — a formulaic phrase of eschatological restoration used across the prophetic corpus (Jer 30:3; 33:7; Ezek 39:25; Amos 9:14). The phrase literally means “turn the turning/captivity” — whether shĕvut comes from shavah (captivity) or shuv (returning) is debated; the MKT follows the majority reading of “restore the fortunes.” The temporal marker “in those days and at that time” links ch. 3 to the Spirit-outpouring of 2:28-32, placing all of ch. 3 in the eschatological horizon.
2: wĕqibbatsthi ʾet kol haggoyim wĕhoridat im ʾel ʾemeq Yĕhoshaphat: “I will gather all the nations and bring them down to the Valley of Jehoshaphat; and I will enter into judgment with them there.” ʾEmeq Yĕhoshaphat (Valley of Jehoshaphat) — the name means “YHWH judges” (YHWH + shaphat = to judge). This is the only occurrence of this place name in the Hebrew Bible, and it was likely a theological name for the site of eschatological judgment rather than an existing geographic location. Later Christian tradition identified it with the Kidron Valley between Jerusalem and the Mount of Olives; 4th-century CE sources reflect this identification. ʿAl ʿammi wĕnachalati Yisraʾel (on behalf of my people and my heritage Israel) — the nations are judged specifically for their treatment of Israel, YHWH’s nachalah (inheritance, heritage).
3: wĕʾet ʿammi yarvu laggoral wĕyittĕnu hayyeled bazzanah wĕhayaldah maĕru bayyin wayyishthu: “For my people they have cast lots; they have traded a boy for a prostitute, and sold a girl for wine and drunk it.” The slave trade described — trading human beings for a prostitute’s services or for wine — reflects ancient Near Eastern slave markets following military conquest. The dehumanization of Israel’s children to the level of commodities exchangeable for a single night’s entertainment is the specific indictment against the nations. Casting lots (yarvu goral) for slaves was standard practice after conquest (cf. Nahum 3:10; Obadiah 11).
4: wĕgam mah ʾattem li Tsor wĕTsidon wĕkol gĕlilot Pĕleshet: “But you — what are you to me, O Tyre and Sidon, and all the regions of Philistia?” The rhetorical question mah ʾattem li (what are you to me?) expresses divine contempt — the same formula in Jeremiah 2:18 and John 2:4. Tyre, Sidon, and Philistia are specifically accused because they were the slave-trading intermediaries who profited from Judah’s conquest. ʾIm gĕmul ʾattem meshalĕmim ʿalay (if you are paying me back for something) — the nations are challenging YHWH through their treatment of Israel; they have made the conflict personal.
5: ʾasher kaspi wĕzahavi lĕqachthem wĕmachemadai hatovim haveʾtem lĕheikhaleikem: “For you have taken my silver and my gold, and have carried my rich treasures into your temples.” The silver and gold of Israel are YHWH’s silver and gold — the possessive frames the plundering of Israel as robbery from YHWH himself. Heikhaleikem (your temples/palaces) — the heikal (great hall) refers to either the inner sanctuary of a temple or a royal palace; in both cases the Israelite valuables have been incorporated into the cult or treasury of the foreign deity. The plundering of sacred objects was a declaration that the victor’s god had defeated the loser’s god.
6: uvĕnei Yĕhudah uvĕnei Yĕrushalayyim mĕkartem livĕnei hayyĕvanim: “You have sold the people of Judah and Jerusalem to the Greeks (Yĕvanim).” Yĕvanim (Greeks) = Ionians, the Greek-speaking peoples (cf. Isa 66:19; Ezek 27:13 where Javan appears in trade lists). The sale of Judean slaves to the Greeks is historically documented: Greek slave markets received captives from Phoenician traders throughout the eastern Mediterranean from at least the 8th century BCE. This verse is one of the arguments for a post-exilic date for Joel: Greek slave trade with the Levant was particularly prominent in the 5th-4th centuries BCE.
7: hinnĕni mĕʿiram min hammaqqom ʾasher mĕkartem ʾotam sham: “Behold, I will rouse them from the place to which you sold them.” YHWH announces a reversal of the slave trade: those who were sold to distant places will be raised up and returned. Wĕhashivoti gĕmulĕkhem bĕroʾshĕkhem (I will return your payment on your own heads) — the principle of lex talionis applied to national commerce: what you did to Israel will be done to you, bĕroʾshĕkhem (on your own heads), the Hebrew idiom for personal accountability (cf. Ezek 16:43; 1 Kgs 2:44).
8: umakharti ʾet bĕneikhem uvĕnoteikem bĕyad bĕnei Yĕhudah: “I will sell your sons and your daughters into the hands of the people of Judah, and they will sell them to the Sabeans.” The Sabeans (Shĕvaʾim) = the people of Sheba in southern Arabia (modern Yemen), known in the ancient world as long-distance traders (cf. 1 Kgs 10; Job 6:19; Isa 60:6). The reversal is exact: those who sold Judeans to distant traders will themselves be sold to a distant trading nation — the eschatological reversal of the slave trade that mirrors the prophetic pattern of nations receiving what they inflicted on Israel (cf. Obadiah; Nahum).
9: qirĕʾu zoʾt baggoyim qaddĕshu milchamah haʿiru haggibbourim yiggeshu yaʿalu kol ʾanshei hammilchamah: “Proclaim this among the nations: Prepare for war! Rouse the mighty men!” Qaddĕshu milchamah (consecrate/prepare for war) — the verb qiddash (to sanctify, consecrate) is applied to war: holy war in the ANE involved cultic preparation, including animal sacrifice, divination, ritual purity, and prophetic consultation before battle. Joel ironically applies this sacred vocabulary to the nations’ mobilization for the battle they will lose. The military roster — gibbourim (mighty men), ʾanshei hammilchamah (men of war) — assembles the full military force of the nations for YHWH’s tribunal.
10: kuttu ʾitĕkhem lĕcharavot wĕmizmaroteikhem lirĕmachim: “Beat your plowshares into swords and your pruning hooks into spears.” This is the deliberate reversal of Isaiah 2:4/Micah 4:3: “they shall beat their swords into plowshares, and their spears into pruning hooks.” Joel 3:10 inverts the eschatological peacetime vision of the other prophets: YHWH is calling the nations to war precisely so that he can defeat them. The plowshare (ʾet) and pruning hook (mazmerah) are transformed into sword (cherev) and spear (romach). Heʾanis ʾemar gibbor ʾani (let the weak say “I am a warrior”) — even those unqualified for battle are summoned; no one is exempted from YHWH’s court.
11: ʿushu wĕvoʾu kol haggoyim miSaviv wĕqibĕtsu shanah: “Hasten and come, all you surrounding nations, and gather yourselves there.” The double summons (ʿush = hasten; vavoʾ = come) creates urgency; YHWH is calling the nations to their own judgment. Hanchit YHWH gibboureikhah (bring down your warriors, O LORD) — a sudden prayer directed to YHWH in the middle of the summons to the nations; the speaker (the prophet? Judah?) calls on YHWH to deploy his own heavenly army (gibbourim). The LXX renders this differently, and the MT is difficult; the MKT follows the MT reading.
12: yeʿoru wĕyaʿalu haggoyim ʾel ʿemeq Yĕhoshaphat ki sham ʾeshev lishpot ʾet kol haggoyim missaviv: “Let the nations be roused and come up to the Valley of Jehoshaphat; for there I will sit to judge all the surrounding nations.” ʾEshev lishpot (I will sit to judge) — YHWH seated as judge (shaphat) is the formal posture of judicial authority in the ancient world (cf. Ps 9:4, 7; Isa 16:5). The Valley of Jehoshaphat (“YHWH judges”) is where YHWH sits to judge — the name and the action are the same: the judgment-place is where YHWH-who-judges presides.
13: shilchu magal ki basal qatsir boʾu redu ki malʾah gat shiqqi gĕdĕshu: “Put in the sickle, for the harvest is ripe. Go in and tread, for the winepress is full.” The two harvest images — grain harvest (sickle, magal) and grape harvest (winepress, gat) — are used for the harvest of nations in judgment. The sickle in the harvest appears in Revelation 14:14-20, where the Son of Man harvests the earth and the angel harvests the grapes of wrath. Ki rabbah raʿatam (for their wickedness is great) — the fullness of the winepress = the fullness of the nations’ wickedness, which has reached the point requiring divine action.
14: hamonim hamonim bĕʿemeq hecharuts ki qarov yom YHWH bĕʿemeq hecharuts: “Multitudes, multitudes in the valley of decision! For the day of the LORD is near in the valley of decision.” Hamonim hamonim (multitudes, multitudes) — the doubled noun for emphasis; the same root as hamon (noise, tumult, crowd) that runs through Ezekiel’s oracles against the nations. ʿEmeq hecharuts (valley of decision/verdict) — charutz means “incised, decisive, sharp” (from charats = to cut, determine); the “decision” is YHWH’s verdict, not human free choice — the valley is named for the decisiveness of the divine judgment that takes place there. The repetition of the phrase creates a solemn cadence marking the climax of the Day of the LORD.
15: shemesh wĕyareach qadaru wĕkochavim ʾasĕfu nogĕham: “The sun and the moon grow dark, and the stars withdraw their shining.” The cosmic darkness of 2:10 is reprised at the eschatological climax: the same cosmic signs surround both the locust invasion (which introduces the Day of the LORD) and the final judgment of the nations. This repetition reinforces Joel’s typological structure: the locust invasion is a historical type of the final Day of the LORD, and its cosmic accompaniments prefigure the final cosmic upheaval.
16: wĕYHWH miTsiyon yishʾag umirushalayyim yitten qolo wĕraʿashu shamayim waʾarets: “The LORD roars from Zion and utters his voice from Jerusalem; the heavens and the earth shake.” This verse is nearly identical to Amos 1:2 (“The LORD roars from Zion and utters his voice from Jerusalem; the pastures of the shepherds mourn...”), suggesting a shared prophetic tradition. Yishʾag (roars, from shaʾag = the roar of a lion) positions YHWH as the lion whose roar terrorizes the nations (cf. Amos 3:8). Wĕmachseh livĕnei Yisraʾel (but the LORD is a refuge for his people, a stronghold for the people of Israel) — the same divine action that destroys the nations is the protection of Israel: YHWH’s terrifying roar is simultaneously the war-cry that defeats the enemy and the lion’s roar that protects the pride.
17: widaʿtem ki ʾani YHWH ʾeloheikhem shoken bĕTsiyon har qodshi: “So you shall know that I am the LORD your God, who dwells in Zion, my holy mountain.” The recognition formula (wĕyĕdaʿtem ki ʾani YHWH) reaches its eschatological conclusion: the nations’ defeat produces Israel’s knowledge of YHWH. YHWH ʾeloheikhem shoken bĕTsiyon (the LORD your God, who dwells in Zion) — divine indwelling (shakan, the root of shekinah) in Zion as the eschatological reality; cf. Ezekiel 48:35 (“the LORD is there”) and Revelation 21:3 (“the dwelling of God is with man”). Wĕyĕrushalayyim tihyeh qodesh (Jerusalem shall be holy) = the consecration of the city that makes it truly the city of YHWH.
18: wĕhayah bayom hahu yittĕfu heharim ʿasis wĕhagĕvaʿot telnah chalav: “And in that day the mountains shall drip with sweet wine, and the hills shall flow with milk.” The eschatological abundance — mountains dripping wine (ʿasis), hills flowing with milk, all the wadis of Judah flowing with water — is the anti-image of the locust devastation of ch. 1. Wĕmayon mibbeit YHWH yetse wĕhishqah ʾet nachal haShittim (a fountain shall come forth from the house of the LORD and water the Wadi of Shittim) — the life-giving stream flowing from the temple is a key eschatological image: Ezekiel 47:1-12 develops it most fully; Zechariah 14:8 and Revelation 22:1-2 carry it into the NT’s vision of the New Jerusalem. Shittim was the last Israelite camp before crossing into Canaan (Num 25:1) — a site of apostasy and death; the fountain from YHWH’s house brings life even to that place of failure.
19: Mitsrayim lishĕmamah tihyeh wĕʾEdom lĕmidbar shĕmamah tihyeh: “Egypt shall become a desolation and Edom a desolate wilderness.” Egypt and Edom are named as the paradigmatic enemies of Israel: Egypt (the house of slavery) and Edom (the brother-nation that turned against Judah at the time of Jerusalem’s fall; cf. Ps 137:7; Obadiah 10-14). The specific charge — meias chamas livĕnei Yĕhudah ʾasher shaphĕku dam naqiʾ bĕʾartsam (for the violence done to the people of Judah, in whose land they shed innocent blood) — grounds the national judgment in specific historical crimes. Dam naqiʾ (innocent blood) is the standard phrase for unjust killing that cries out for divine vengeance (Gen 4:10; Deut 19:10; Jer 19:4).
20: wĕYĕhudah lĕʿolam teshev wĕYĕrushalayyim lĕdor wador: “But Judah shall be inhabited forever, and Jerusalem to all generations.” The eschatological permanence of Judah and Jerusalem stands in absolute contrast to the desolation of Egypt and Edom. Lĕʿolam (forever) and lĕdor wador (to all generations) — the two Hebrew expressions for permanence. This is the positive fulfillment of the Abrahamic and Davidic covenants: the land shall be possessed forever (Gen 17:8; 2 Sam 7:16). The NT rereads “Jerusalem” and “Zion” in terms of the heavenly Jerusalem (Gal 4:26; Heb 12:22; Rev 21).
21: wĕniqqeti damam lo niqqeti wĕYHWH shoken bĕTsiyon: “I will avenge their blood — the blood I have not yet avenged — for the LORD dwells in Zion.” The final verse of Joel is syntactically difficult: wĕniqqeti damam lo niqqeti may mean (1) “I will cleanse/avenge their blood which I have not yet cleansed/avenged” (taking niqqah as “to avenge”); or (2) “I will declare innocent their blood which I have declared not innocent” (treating the statement as a declaration of innocence for Israel’s shed blood). The MKT follows reading (1): YHWH promises to execute vengeance for the innocent blood shed against his people — the eschatological justice that the historical present has not yet seen. The book closes with the ground of hope: wĕYHWH shoken bĕTsiyon (the LORD dwells in Zion) — YHWH’s presence in Zion is the guarantee of his people’s future.
